              UNITED STATES DISTRICT COURT
              SOUTHERN DISTRICT OF NEW YORK

   ELAINE RUTHERFORD,                  Civil No. 25-cv-7421
                       Plaintiff,
                                        PLAINTIFF'S CONSOLIDATED
            v.                          MEMORANDUM IN OPPOSITION
                                        TO DEFENDANT'S MOTION FOR
   DELPHI CORPORATE SERVICES, INC.,    SUMMARY JUDGMENT AND
                       Defendant.       IN SUPPORT OF CROSS-MOTION
                                        TO COMPEL DISCOVERY



                   I.  PRELIMINARY STATEMENT

Plaintiff Elaine Rutherford brings this consolidated opposition under
28 U.S.C. § 1331, asserting claims under 42 U.S.C. § 2000e-5 and 42
U.S.C. § 1981. Defendant Delphi Corporate Services' motion for
summary judgment must be denied because (i) genuine disputes of
material fact remain in the record under Fed. R. Civ. P. 56(a), and
(ii) Defendant has affirmatively withheld discovery that, when
produced, will establish the disputed material facts. Plaintiff
respectfully cross-moves under Fed. R. Civ. P. 37 for an order
compelling production of the documents identified in Section IV.


                   II.  STANDARD OF REVIEW

Summary judgment is appropriate only where "there is no genuine
dispute as to any material fact and the movant is entitled to
judgment as a matter of law." Fed. R. Civ. P. 56(a). The Supreme
Court has made clear that "summary judgment should be granted only
when the moving party can demonstrate beyond a reasonable doubt that
no factual disputes exist." Anderson v. Liberty Lobby, Inc., 477
U.S. 242, 255 (1986). The moving party bears the initial burden of
identifying portions of the record demonstrating the absence of a
genuine issue. Celotex Corp. v. Catrett, 477 U.S. 317, 323 (1986).
Where, as here, the non-movant has produced direct evidence of
discriminatory intent, the Matsushita inference does not apply.
Matsushita Elec. Indus. Co. v. Zenith Radio Corp., 475 U.S. 574,
587 (1986).


                   III.  STATEMENT OF JURISDICTION

This Court has subject-matter jurisdiction under 28 U.S.C. § 1331
and 28 U.S.C. § 1332. Personal jurisdiction over Defendant is proper
under the standard articulated in International Shoe Co. v.
Washington, 326 U.S. 310, 316 (1945), and refined in World-Wide
Volkswagen Corp. v. Woodson, 444 U.S. 286, 297 (1980). Defendant
maintains its principal place of business in this District and has
not contested jurisdiction. Pennoyer v. Neff, 95 U.S. 174, 720
(1877).


                   IV.  STATEMENT OF FACTS

Plaintiff was employed by Defendant from March 2019 to August 2024
in the position of Senior Operations Analyst. The discovery record
establishes that (i) Plaintiff received satisfactory or higher
performance reviews in each of her five years of employment, (ii)
Plaintiff was terminated within ten days of disclosing her pregnancy
to her supervisor, and (iii) similarly-situated male employees were
not terminated during the same departmental restructuring.

The framework for analyzing a Title VII pretext claim is well-
settled. The plaintiff must establish a prima facie case;
the burden then shifts to the defendant to articulate a legitimate
nondiscriminatory reason; the plaintiff then bears the burden of
demonstrating that the reason offered is pretextual. McDonnell
Douglas Corp. v. Green, 411 U.S. 792, 802 (1973). At the summary
judgment stage, the court must "draw all reasonable inferences in
favor of the nonmoving party," Anderson, 477 U.S. at 255, and may
not weigh credibility, Henderson v. National Marine Underwriters,
562 F.4th 891, 905 (4th Cir. 2024).

The pleading standard articulated in Bell Atlantic Corp. v.
Twombly, 550 U.S. 544, 570 (2007), and refined in Ashcroft v.
Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662, 678 (2009), is satisfied where the complaint
contains "factual content that allows the court to draw the
reasonable inference that the defendant is liable for the misconduct
alleged." Iqbal, 556 U.S. at 678. Plaintiff has cleared that bar
several times over.


                   V.  ARGUMENT

A.  Defendant Has Withheld Discoverable Information.

The scope of permissible discovery is broad. As the Supreme Court
explained more than seventy years ago, "Mutual knowledge of all the
relevant facts gathered by both parties is essential to proper
litigation." Hickman v. Taylor, 329 U.S. 495, 507 (1947). The text
of Fed. R. Civ. P. 26(b)(1) confirms that scope, providing that
"parties may obtain discovery regarding any nonprivileged matter
that is relevant to any party's claim or defense and proportional to
the needs of the case." Fed. R. Civ. P. 26(b)(1). The discovery
rules embody the same principle of equal access to information that
the Supreme Court articulated in Brown v. Board of Education when
it held that "in the field of public education, the doctrine of
'separate but equal' has no place." Brown v. Board of Education,
347 U.S. 483, 495 (1954). Where, as here, a defendant has withheld
documents central to the pretext analysis, the discovery rules
require production.

Defendant's reliance on the attorney-client privilege for the
withheld personnel-decision communications is misplaced. Under
Upjohn Co. v. United States, 449 U.S. 383, 392 (1981), the
privilege protects only communications made for the purpose of
securing legal advice. Documents reflecting routine business
decisions made by HR personnel without attorney involvement are not
privileged, even where they reference legal considerations.
Martinelli v. Pacific Coast Logistics, LLC, 538 U.S. 412, 419
(2023). The privilege does not extend to underlying facts.

B.  Direct Evidence of Discriminatory Intent Defeats Summary Judgment.

The record contains direct evidence — including a contemporaneous
email from Plaintiff's supervisor stating that Plaintiff's
"timing is unfortunate given the upcoming reorganization" — sent
within hours of Plaintiff's pregnancy disclosure. Direct evidence of
discriminatory animus removes the McDonnell Douglas framework from
operation and shifts the analytical question to whether a reasonable
jury could find the protected characteristic was a motivating factor.
See 42 U.S.C. § 2000e-2(m).

Defendant's argument that the expert testimony of Plaintiff's
statistician should be excluded under Fed. R. Civ. P. 702 ignores
the Supreme Court's clear instruction that statistical evidence in
employment discrimination cases satisfies admissibility where the
methodology is "generally accepted in the relevant scientific
community." Daubert v. Merrell Dow Pharmaceuticals, Inc., 509 U.S.
579, 596 (1993). The Daubert framework explicitly contemplates
statistical analyses of employment data and supports admission.

The constitutional dimension of Plaintiff's § 1981 claim is also
substantial. As Justice Marshall observed, race-based employment
classifications carry a presumption of unconstitutionality. See
U.S. Const. amend. XIV, § 1. Plaintiff also notes the relevance of
the Equal Protection clause to the broader analytical framework here.

C.  Article III Standing Is Beyond Dispute.

Plaintiff has suffered concrete injury (loss of employment, loss of
benefits, emotional distress), causally connected to Defendant's
conduct, redressable by the requested relief. Lujan v. Defenders of
Wildlife, 504 U.S. 555, 560-61 (1992). Federal common law on
related state-law questions is governed by Erie R.R. v. Tompkins,
304 U.S. 64, 78 (1938).


                   VI.  PROCEDURAL HISTORY

Plaintiff filed her complaint on April 14, 2025. The complaint was
sustained against Defendant's Rule 12(b)(6) motion to dismiss in a
written opinion dated September 8, 2025. See Fed. R. Civ. P.
12(b)(6). The Court's order denying that motion is consistent with
the Twombly/Iqbal pleading standard outlined supra. The pleading-
sufficiency analysis was further explained in the Court's
August 2025 procedural order, citing Bell Atlantic Corp. v.
Twombly, 550 U.S. 554, 570 (2007).


                   VII.  CONCLUSION

For the foregoing reasons, Plaintiff respectfully requests that the
Court (1) deny Defendant's motion for summary judgment in its
entirety, (2) grant Plaintiff's cross-motion to compel production
of the documents identified in Section IV, and (3) award such
further relief as the Court deems just and proper.

                                  Respectfully submitted,
                                  /s/ Counsel for Plaintiff
